game_genre_context: Social mobile game with friend interactions (Clash Royale, Brawl Stars). Friend-action notifications ('Alex sent you a battle invite') drive 40% of session starts. Get bundling wrong and friends become spam sources; get it right and friend notifications become social glue.
title: Friend-Action Notification Digester with Personalization and Per-Sender Throttling
design_intent: Build a friend-notification system that bundles per-sender ('Alex sent 3 invites' instead of 3 pings), throttles per friend to 2/day max (prevents one over-eager friend from dominating), and personalizes copy with friend's display name + relationship tier. Snapchat figured this out at scale — friend pings are emotional, not transactional. Mishandle this and players mute friends, killing social retention.
mathematical_foundation: Per-sender throttle: max 2 notifications per friend-pair per 24h. Digest window: 30min — multiple actions from same friend collapsed into one notification. Notification priority by relationship tier: best_friend = 100, ultra = 80, great = 60, good = 40. If queue contains multiple senders, send highest-priority single notification + digest of rest. Time-of-day relevance: friend invites suppressed during recipient's sleep window unless flagged as 'live event' (e.g., raid starting now).
psychology_notes: Friend pings feel personal — name + face + recent context. Bundled digest preserves curiosity without overwhelming. Per-sender throttle protects friendships (you don't want to be that friend whose notifications get muted). Tier-based prioritization respects that 'best friend' interactions are higher signal than 'good friend' ones.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Friend action (invite, gift, message) triggers notification request. System checks throttle bucket for (sender, recipient) pair. If under 2/day cap, evaluates digest opportunity — if previous notification from same sender in last 30min, merge. Else send immediately with personalized copy.

2. **Math**: 2/day cap derived from internal testing — beyond 2/day from one friend, recipient mute rate jumps 4x. 30-min digest window matches expected human session boundary.

3. **Edge cases**: New friend (just added) — first notification always goes through, subsequent throttled. Friend changes display name mid-digest — use current name. Sender blocked between trigger and send — drop silently.

4. **Visual**: In-app notification with sender avatar + relationship tier badge. Digest displayed as 'Alex sent 3 invites · tap to see all'. Tier badge on push (best friend = gold border).

5. **Anti-exploit**: Throttle prevents harassment-by-notification. Block functionality severs all future pings.

6. **Rejected**: Per-event push (spam), global friend cap (too restrictive for active groups), no personalization (sterile).

database_design.schema: friend_notif_throttle: (sender_id, recipient_id, date) PK, sent_count INT, last_sent_at. friend_notification_log: id, sender_id, recipient_id, action_type, bundled_count, sent_at.
database_design.denormalization: Friend relationship tier cached in notification payload at send time.
database_design.transactions: Throttle check + send atomic via SELECT FOR UPDATE on throttle row.

visualization_strategy: Avatar frame color indicates tier — best friend gets gold border. Bundle indicator in yellow shows aggregation. Tier-aware copy ('Your best friend wants to battle') creates relational warmth. Reduced motion: no animations needed. Color-blind: tier indicated by border thickness + text label.
balance_considerations: Watch mute rate per friend pair — if >5%, throttle too generous. Watch open rate per tier (best friend should be 70%+, good friend 30%+). Watch unfriend rate post-notification — high = harassment vector working as bug.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Notification harassment — 2/day cap prevents spam.
Auto-bot sending invites — same cap + behavioral detection of bot-sender patterns.
Bundle exploitation (queuing many actions to display as 'big' notification) — bundle count capped at 5 displayed.
observability: Log all friend notifications with throttle hits, bundle merges. Metrics: per-tier open rate, mute rate, unfriend correlation.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior sends push per friend action. One eager friend sends 30 invites overnight, recipient wakes to 30 pings, mutes friend permanently, social retention dies.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Snapchat friend pings (impeccable bundling), Pokemon Go raid invites. DONE BADLY: early Words With Friends — turn-notifications spammed beyond reasonable cadence, players turned off friend pings entirely.

game_genre_context: Web MMORPG (RuneScape, ArcheAge Web) where quest chains span multiple days. Multi-day quests with branching choices feel like stories, not chores. WoW's daily story quests during Legion expansion drove unprecedented engagement.
title: Multi-Day Quest Chain System with Branching Choices and Time-Gated Story Beats
design_intent: Build a quest chain system where day 1's choice affects day 2's available quests, with time-gates between chapters (next chapter unlocks 18h after current completes — daily appointment), and branching narrative paths (3-5 endings). Players who complete chain get title + cosmetic. WoW's quest chains, Genshin's Archon Quests, Star Wars: The Old Republic's class stories — all use this pattern. The story IS the engagement.
mathematical_foundation: Chain length 5-7 chapters, 18h gate between (creates daily 'next chapter ready' moment). Branching factor 2-3 choices per chapter; total path combinations = product of branches = 32-243 unique stories per chain. Reward escalation: chapter_N reward = base * (1.5)^N. Completion gives cosmetic + title; players who complete share screenshots = organic marketing.
psychology_notes: Time-gates create anticipation (counter ticking down = 'just one more day'). Branching choices give agency, but more importantly create CONVERSATIONS ('which ending did you get?'). Multi-day commitment = sunk cost = retention. Story-based progression respects players who play for narrative, not grind.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player starts chain. Chapter 1 quest available immediately. Complete → choice modal → next chapter scheduled for now + 18h. At time-gate expiry, next chapter unlocks. Branches stored as state — quest content adapts.

2. **Math**: 18h gate aligns to next-day-same-time activity pattern (slightly less than 24h so chain doesn't drift later each day).

3. **Edge cases**: Player skips a day — chapter remains available, no decay. Choices irreversible to preserve narrative weight. Player completes all branches across multiple playthroughs — multi-ending tracking.

4. **Visual**: Chain progression as visual roadmap with completed/locked/active nodes. Choice modal styled cinematically. Time-gate countdown prominent.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Time gates server-enforced. Choice once-locked-never-changed.

6. **Rejected**: Linear no-choice quests (boring), instant unlocks (kills appointment loop), reversible choices (kills weight).

database_design.schema: quest_chains: id, code, chapters_total. chapter_defs: chain_id, chapter_num, content JSONB, choices JSONB. player_chain_progress: player_id, chain_id, current_chapter, choices_made INT[], chapter_unlocks_at, completed_at.
database_design.denormalization: Choices stored as array on progress doc.

visualization_strategy: Chain roadmap shows journey at-a-glance — completed nodes solid green, current cyan pulsing, future dimmed. Time-gate countdown big and cinematic — appointment feel. Choice modal warns 'cannot be reversed' — weight communicated. Reduced motion: no pulse, static cyan. Color-blind: state indicated by checkmark/dot icon.
balance_considerations: Watch chapter completion rate — drop-off identifies weak chapters. Watch ending distribution — should be roughly balanced (33/33/33) unless one path is intentionally rare. Watch return rate at time-gate expiry — high = appointment loop working.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Choice manipulation post-submit — choices appended atomically, never editable.
Time gate skip via clock manipulation — server time only.
Chain replay for multiple endings — separate chain instance per playthrough, prior choices irrelevant.
observability: Log chapter_completed, choice_made, chain_completed_with_ending. Metrics: chapter funnel, ending distribution, completion rate.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior makes 'daily quest with story flavor text' — players ignore story. Then: instant unlock between chapters — no appointment loop. Then: no branching — players who got 'bad ending' have no replay value. SW:TOR class stories did this right; many modern attempts botch the time-gating.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Star Wars: The Old Republic class stories, Genshin Archon Quests, WoW Suramar chain. DONE BADLY: Destiny 2 launch story — no branching, no replay value, players blew through in 6 hours and quit. Bungie eventually rebuilt the narrative system.

game_genre_context: MMO with bind-on-pickup raid loot (WoW raids, Final Fantasy XIV raids). 20-40 players kill boss, loot distributed via raid leader's chosen system. Bad loot systems split guilds; good ones (personal loot, group-loot, master-loot variants) keep raid teams together.
title: Hybrid Personal-Loot + Group-Loot Raid System with Trade Window
design_intent: Build a raid loot system where boss drops generate per-player personal loot (no contention) PLUS group-loot extras (rolls), with a 2-hour trade window where personal loot can be given to a guildmate if you have an item already. WoW post-Legion uses this exact pattern; it solved decade-long loot drama. The trade window is the genius: gear feels personal but isn't selfish.
mathematical_foundation: Boss drops N_personal items (one per X players based on raid size) + M_group items rolled on. Loot table: each player has affix_pool for their class. Personal drop chance per player per boss = 0.15-0.25 (tuned per content tier). Item level scales with boss difficulty. Trade window: 2h to gift personal loot to guildmate (requires raid attendance signature).
psychology_notes: Personal loot removes 'someone stole MY drop' rage. Trade window adds back generosity gameplay ('you need this more, take it') without forcing it. Group-loot extras for special items preserves community moments (everyone wants the legendary mount). Hybrid is best-of-both.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Boss dies → server rolls per-player personal loot (with affix). Boss also has group_loot_pool of special items → rolls among interested players. Personal loot goes to inventory but is tradeable for 2h within raid group.

2. **Math**: Personal drop rate ~0.15 per boss per player keeps Tier set acquisition reasonable over a tier (10-12 raid weeks).

3. **Edge cases**: Player DCs mid-fight — still gets loot if alive at kill. Player AFKs entire fight — loot rules vary (FFXIV allows; WoW removes if no damage dealt). Trade attempted to non-raid-member — blocked.

4. **Visual**: Loot rolls displayed live in chat. Personal loot popup shows what dropped + 'trade available' indicator. 'Group loot' panel shows extras with roll buttons.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Server-authoritative rolls. Trade requires both consent. Trade window auto-expires.

6. **Rejected**: Pure master-loot (loot drama), pure personal-loot (no trade = item stuck on wrong player), pure group-loot (toxic rolling).

database_design.schema: raid_kills: id, raid_id, boss_id, killed_at, participants UUID[]. raid_loot: id, raid_kill_id, player_id, item_id, is_personal BOOLEAN, traded_to NULL, trade_expires_at, claimed BOOLEAN. raid_group_loot: id, raid_kill_id, item_id, eligible_players UUID[], roll_winner.
database_design.transactions: Loot generation single transaction; trade single transaction with both-consent check.

visualization_strategy: Personal drops shown with class-color border (recognizable game-wide). Trade timer ticks down (urgency without panic). Group roll button explicit ('Need'/'Pass'). Reduced motion: instant transitions.
balance_considerations: Watch drop-rate per boss (target 15-25% per player). Watch trade rate per drop (10-20% expected — generous community). Watch ninja-trade incidents (player trades to wrong person 'by accident') — UI confirmation prevents.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Trade after window closes — server time check.
Trade to non-raid member — participant check.
Loot exploit via DC + reconnect — alive-at-kill check authoritative.
Multi-account farming with alts — alt detection at raid composition validation.
observability: Log all drops, trades, rolls. Metrics: avg drops per raid, trade rate, item-class match rate.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: master loot only. Drama. Guild splits. Then: no trade window, mistakes irreversible. Then: pure group roll, raid time consumed by /roll spam. WoW's loot system evolution is a 15-year case study.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: WoW Personal Loot + Trade (post-Legion gold standard), FFXIV Greed/Need rolling. DONE BADLY: WoW Vanilla master-loot abuse — leaders gave best items to friends/themselves, guild collapses. Lesson: removing human discretion removes human drama.

game_genre_context: Hero shooter / MOBA (Overwatch 2, Apex Legends) where battle pass intersects with hero balance — characters unlocked through battle pass affect competitive meta. Apex Legends gates new Legends behind pass tiers, sparking debates about pay-to-win.
title: Battle Pass with Free Hero Unlock Track and Skin-Only Premium Tier
design_intent: Build a battle pass structured so ALL gameplay-affecting content (new heroes, balance-relevant gear) is on the free track, with premium track being purely cosmetic (skins, emotes, voice lines). This separates monetization from pay-to-win accusations. Overwatch 1 had this; Overwatch 2 reversed it (heroes behind pass) and faced massive backlash. Apex Legends has hybrid (heroes purchasable with grind currency too).
mathematical_foundation: Free track: 50 tiers including new hero at tier 30 (achievable with normal play in ~3 weeks of 90-day season). Premium track: 100 tiers with cosmetics. Premium tier rewards roughly 3x cosmetic value vs free. XP curve identical for both tracks (just rewards differ).
psychology_notes: Premium-only-cosmetics positioning: 'pay for vanity, not power' — builds trust. Hero on free track at tier 30 = achievable but requires playing = drives engagement. Premium track tier 100 cosmetic = ultimate cosmetic flex = whale bait.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Standard battle pass mechanics. Distinction: rewards tagged 'cosmetic' (skins) vs 'gameplay' (heroes). Free track contains all gameplay rewards. Premium track all cosmetic. XP earned same way.

2. **Math**: Tier 30 unlock for new hero positions it as 'engaged players get the meta' without 'must pay for new meta'.

3. **Edge cases**: Player who buys premium late mid-season — retroactively grants all cosmetic tiers earned. Player who never buys premium still gets new hero. Player at tier 100 free — no further rewards on free, premium track still has cosmetics.

4. **Visual**: Two tracks clearly labeled 'Gameplay' (free) and 'Cosmetics' (premium). Hero unlock at tier 30 highlighted.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Standard battle pass anti-exploit.

6. **Rejected**: New hero on premium track (pay-to-win backlash), free track empty (no F2P retention).

database_design.schema: Extends battle pass with reward_category ENUM(cosmetic, gameplay, currency). New hero unlock as gameplay reward at specific tier.

frontend_solution:
// src/components/EthicalBattlePass.tsx — emphasizes track distinction export function EthicalBattlePass({ data }: any) { return (
✓ No pay-to-win
All new heroes and gameplay items unlock on the free track. Premium is cosmetics only.
Free · Gameplay
Premium · Cosmetics only
{data.tiers.map((t: any) => (
{t.tier}
{t.free?.name} {t.free?.category === 'gameplay' && GAMEPLAY}
{t.premium?.name} cosmetic
))}
); }
visualization_strategy: 'No pay-to-win' banner prominent — trust signal. Side-by-side free vs premium tracks with category labels — players immediately see the split. Reduced motion: no animations. Color-blind: track distinction via icons + position.
balance_considerations: Watch new hero pickup rate by player segment — if F2P pickup <60%, free unlock too late. Watch premium conversion — if cosmetic-only premium converts <8%, premium not valuable enough. Watch competitive integrity discussions on social.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Hero purchase via direct currency to skip pass — disabled or priced equivalently to discourage shortcut.
Cosmetic-as-stat-buff — schema enforced (no premium gameplay rewards).
observability: Track hero unlock progression, premium conversion, social sentiment (no P2W complaints).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior puts new hero on premium track. Pay-to-win backlash. Refund storm. Subreddit on fire. Overwatch 2 launched this way; took 4 seasons to roll back partially. Trust never fully recovered.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Overwatch 1 (heroes free for all). DONE BADLY: Overwatch 2 launch (heroes behind pass tier 55, then nerfed). Lesson: separate fun from money; players pay more for cosmetics if they trust you on gameplay.

game_genre_context: Real-time .io style web game (Agar.io, Slither.io, Surviv.io) with hundreds of players per server in shared world. State delta sync, interest management (only see players nearby), and lag compensation are existential.
title: Spatial Interest Management with Quadtree-Based Visibility Culling for 500-Player Server
design_intent: Build a server-authoritative game loop that handles 500 concurrent players in shared world, sending each client ONLY the state of nearby entities (interest management via quadtree). Without this, each client receives 500*60Hz state updates = network meltdown. Agar.io and Surviv.io use this exact pattern. The technique scales by sending O(log N) updates per player instead of O(N).
mathematical_foundation: Quadtree spatial index: O(log N) lookup for entities within visibility radius. Visibility radius = 1.5 * screen size. Server tick rate 30Hz; client interpolates at 60Hz. Delta compression: only send entities that changed since last tick to that client (set difference). Bandwidth budget: ~5KB/sec per client target.
psychology_notes: Smooth gameplay = retention. Stuttering = uninstall. Players don't think about interest management but it's why .io games work on mobile data. Lag compensation makes the game FEEL responsive even on 100ms ping.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Server tick: update all entities, rebuild quadtree, for each client query nearby entities, diff vs last sent, send delta. Client receives delta, applies, interpolates between ticks.

2. **Math**: Quadtree depth limited to ~6 levels for 500 entities. Each query is fast. Delta compression keeps payload tiny.

3. **Edge cases**: Player moves rapidly across map — new entities pop in, may need to send 'full state' for newly visible. Player at edge of visibility — hysteresis (don't pop in/out repeatedly).

4. **Visual**: Smooth interpolation hides 30Hz server rate. Entities outside visibility simply don't exist client-side.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Client can't see hidden entities (server filters). Wallhacks impossible because data not sent.

6. **Rejected**: Send all entities to all clients (network meltdown), polling (latency).

database_design.schema: Game state in-memory only. Persistent: match results, player stats.

visualization_strategy: Players outside visibility don't render — naturally hides them. Smooth interpolation hides server tick rate. Predictive client movement (move immediately on input, reconcile on server update) feels responsive.
balance_considerations: Watch p95 bandwidth per client (target <10KB/s). Watch entity count per client average (target 30-80). Watch interpolation jitter (target <50ms perceived).
exploit_vectors_addressed: Wallhack/maphack — impossible because data not sent for invisible entities.
Speed hack — server-authoritative velocity caps.
Position spoof — server is single source for own position too.
observability: Log per-client bandwidth, tick latency, packet loss. Metrics: server tick duration, max entities per quadtree node.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior broadcasts full state to all. Server CPU melts at 100 players. Mobile clients on 4G can't keep up. Game unplayable. .io games can't exist without spatial culling.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Agar.io, Surviv.io, krunker.io. DONE BADLY: many failed .io clones launched without interest management, died in first weekend due to lag.

game_genre_context: Idle / incremental game (Cookie Clicker, NGU Idle, Idle Heroes) where infinite content depth comes from procedurally generated upgrade paths and prestige resets. The PCG isn't visual — it's mathematical depth.
title: Procedural Skill Tree Generator with Balance-Constrained Synergy Detection
design_intent: Build a skill tree generator that creates 200-node trees with random affix combinations, automatically detects and forbids broken synergies (e.g., two nodes that together double DPS infinitely), and balances node power via constrained optimization. Each prestige run gives a NEW tree, so meta-strategies emerge. Path of Exile's Atlas Passive Tree is rebuilt-feeling each season; idle games like Antimatter Dimensions use procedural prestige trees brilliantly.
mathematical_foundation: Tree as DAG: 200 nodes, each requires 1-3 parent nodes. Node effect = base * multiplier. Synergy detection: for each pair of nodes, simulate equipped state — if final DPS exceeds individual sum by >2x, flag as broken synergy and force them on non-overlapping branches. Constraint solver: ensures total power across tree is normally distributed (no overpowered nodes, no useless ones).
psychology_notes: Procedural trees mean every prestige feels fresh — solved meta becomes stale; new tree forces new strategy. Synergy detection prevents 'cheese builds' that trivialize content. Players love discovering personal optimal paths.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Prestige triggers tree gen. Seeded RNG creates node layout, assigns effects from pool. Constraint solver runs balance pass (rejects bad combos). Tree persisted for run duration.

2. **Math**: 200 nodes is the sweet spot — enough depth for emergent strategy, not so many that exploration paralyzes.

3. **Edge cases**: Generation produces tree with no viable build — constraint check fails, re-roll with seed+1. Player allocates point to about-to-be-nerfed node — locked-in for run, fixed on next prestige.

4. **Visual**: Tree shown as interactive node graph. Allocated nodes glow. Synergy paths visualizable. Reset confirmation explicit.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Tree generation server-side. Allocation server-validated against tree structure.

6. **Rejected**: Fixed tree (solved meta), no balance pass (broken builds dominate).

database_design.schema: player_prestige_tree: player_id, prestige_num, seed, tree JSONB (nodes + edges + effects), generated_at. player_node_allocations: player_id, prestige_num, allocated_node_ids INT[].
database_design.denormalization: Tree stored as JSON to avoid joins.

visualization_strategy: Allocated nodes glow cyan; unlockable nodes lighter; locked dim. Connections light up between allocated nodes. Hover tooltips show effect. Color-blind: allocated indicated by size + glow.
balance_considerations: Watch builds players gravitate toward — if 80% pick same path, generator too predictable. Watch DPS scaling per prestige — should grow but not exponentially.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Allocating without parent — server check.
Generating overpowered tree by seeking specific seeds — random seed, server-only generation.
Modifying client tree display — server is authoritative.
observability: Log tree_generated, node_allocated, prestige_completed. Metrics: average tree depth allocated, build diversity (Gini of pick frequency).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: fixed tree. Solved in week 1, meta dies. Then: random unbalanced — broken builds trivialize, others useless.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Antimatter Dimensions prestige system, NGU Idle. DONE BADLY: many idle games ship fixed trees, peak engagement at 2 weeks then collapse.

game_genre_context: Fitness/exergame (Ring Fit Adventure, Beat Saber, Pokémon Sleep) where stats become a behavior-change tool. Showing 'you exercised 47 days in a row, here's your trend' creates habit.
title: Year-in-Review Scrollytelling Generator with Personal Records and Emotional Highlights
design_intent: Build a 'Wrapped'-style scrollable year-in-review that auto-generates emotional narrative from player stats: 'You spent 47 hours playing', 'Your best month was October', 'Your top opponent was Sarah — you played 23 matches', presented as scroll cards with parallax visuals. Spotify Wrapped is the gold standard, copied across industries. The data itself isn't new — the storytelling is.
mathematical_foundation: Story beats selected via templating against stats thresholds. Card scoring: each candidate insight scored by 'shareability' (rarity, emotional impact). Top N selected. Layout uses CSS scroll-snap. Generated client-side from server-fetched aggregate JSON.
psychology_notes: Narrative arc (intro → peaks → reflection → look-ahead) mirrors movie structure. Personal records create pride. Sharable cards create marketing. Year-in-review = annual ritual = retention.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Year-end trigger generates aggregate stats. Story generator picks top insights. Scroll-card UI displays sequentially. Share button exports to image.

2. **Math**: Story selection via threshold checks (only insights with >X significance shown).

3. **Edge cases**: New player with little data — abbreviated review or skipped. Player on bot-heavy matches — exclude from comparison stats.

4. **Visual**: Each card full-screen, parallax background, big numbers, emotional copy. Auto-advance or scroll. Final card prompts share.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Stats from server-validated data only.

6. **Rejected**: Static stats page (no emotion), forced sharing (annoying).

database_design.schema: player_yearly_aggregate: player_id, year, stats JSONB (matches, hours, top_opponent, etc.).
database_design.denormalization: Pre-computed nightly during last 2 weeks of year.

visualization_strategy: Each card full-screen, snap-scrolling, gradient background rotated per card (visual variety). Numbers HUGE — center of attention. Spring animation on scroll-into-view (delight). Final card prompts share — viral moment. Reduced motion: scroll without spring; gradient still varies.
balance_considerations: Watch completion rate (do players scroll all cards?). Watch share rate (target >15% of viewers share). Card sequence: peaks early to hook, milestones middle, social outro.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Stats from server-validated matches only.
Share image generated server-side to prevent fake stats screenshots being attributed to player.
observability: Log wrapped_viewed, cards_scrolled, share_clicked. Metrics: completion funnel, share rate, year-over-year retention boost from wrapped season.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior shows stats table. No emotion. No shares. Spotify Wrapped is a $$$ marketing engine because it's a story, not a spreadsheet.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Spotify Wrapped (industry-defining), Apple Music Replay, Strava Year in Sport. DONE BADLY: most game stats pages — accurate but boring.

game_genre_context: Esports semi-pro Swiss tournaments (FACEIT, Battlefy events with 64-128 players). Swiss format means everyone plays 5-7 rounds regardless of W/L — better for participation than single elimination.
title: Swiss System Tournament with Buchholz Tiebreaker and Live Round-Pairing
design_intent: Build a Swiss tournament where after each round, players are paired by score with Monrad pairing (avoid rematches), with Buchholz tiebreaker (sum of opponents' final scores) for ties. After 5-7 rounds, top players advance to playoff bracket. Chess.com Swiss tournaments, Magic: The Gathering Pro Tour, large-field StarCraft tournaments all use this exact pattern. The system is mathematically elegant — every player gets full schedule.
mathematical_foundation: Optimal rounds = ceil(log2(N)) for 1 winner; for top-K cut, rounds = ceil(log2(N/K)) + 2. Monrad pairing: within score group, pair top half vs bottom half. Buchholz score = sum(final_score_of_each_opponent). Tiebreaker order: (1) match wins, (2) Buchholz, (3) opponent-Buchholz, (4) head-to-head.
psychology_notes: Swiss respects participants — losing round 1 doesn't eliminate you. Big fields love it. Top-cut to playoff brings drama. Buchholz tiebreaker rewards beating strong opponents (not just luck-of-draw).
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Round 1: pair by seed (top vs bottom). Subsequent rounds: pair within score groups, avoid rematches. After last Swiss round, top K cut to playoff bracket.

2. **Math**: Monrad pairing minimizes rematches. Buchholz computed after final round.

3. **Edge cases**: Odd player count — bye in lowest score group (1 win credit). Forfeit — counted as 0 for player, opponent gets win + bye-equivalent score. Late drop — remaining opponents get byes.

4. **Visual**: Round-by-round pairings displayed. Player can see own bracket + standings live. Buchholz score shown for top players nearing cut.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Pairings generated by trusted algorithm, not admin discretion. Results require both player + admin confirmation.

6. **Rejected**: Random pairings (unfair), pure Swiss without cut (no climactic finals).

database_design.schema: swiss_tournaments: id, total_rounds, current_round, cut_size. swiss_players: tournament_id, player_id, score, buchholz, opponents_played UUID[], byes INT. swiss_matches: tournament_id, round, player_a, player_b, winner, result.
database_design.indexes: swiss_players: (tournament_id, score DESC, buchholz DESC). swiss_matches: (tournament_id, round).
database_design.transactions: Pairing generation single tx; round advance after all results reported.

visualization_strategy: Cut-line clearly marked with 'CUT' tag — players know who's advancing. Above-cut rows green-tinted for clarity. Buchholz column for tiebreaker visibility. Reduced motion: no animations. Color-blind: cut indicated by text tag + position above line.
balance_considerations: Watch dropout rate per round (target <10%). Watch tie frequency requiring Buchholz tiebreak (some ties expected — system working). Optimal round count tied to field size.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Matchmaking manipulation (intentional loss to face weaker) — Swiss pairs within score groups, intentional loss puts you in weaker pool but Buchholz penalizes.
Collusion (split-prize agreement) — Buchholz reveals via Monrad pairing analysis.
Forfeit gaming — forfeit doesn't grant opponent bonus score, can't be exploited.
observability: Log pairings, results, Buchholz changes. Metrics: dropout rate, tie frequency, cut-line drama.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: random pairings — broken. Or no tiebreaker — multiple players at same score, can't determine top cut. MTG had this disaster in 2003 — manual tiebreak chaos.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: chess.com Swiss tournaments, MTG Pro Tour. DONE BADLY: early online poker tournaments — Swiss-like without proper tiebreakers, cut decided by 'shootout' rounds, players furious.

game_genre_context: Co-op survival game (Deep Rock Galactic, Helldivers 2, Phasmophobia) where matchmaking pairs players with similar progression levels for shared content. Mismatched levels = boring for high, deadly for low. The art is creating mixed parties without trivializing or rage-quitting.
title: Co-op Difficulty Matchmaker with Mission-Tier Filter and Carry-Mode Detection
design_intent: Build a co-op matchmaker that filters by mission tier, accepts ±2 level differences within a tier, but detects 'carry parties' (3 max-level + 1 newbie) where newbie can't contribute, redirects newbie to better fit. Helldivers 2's helldive-difficulty matchmaking gates by mission tier; without it, low-level players get insta-killed by high-difficulty enemies and quit.
mathematical_foundation: Tier match required (binary). Level tolerance: ±2 levels in same tier. Carry detection: if std deviation of party levels > 5, flag for review. Mission contribution scoring: predict newbie contribution = level / median_party_level. If <0.3, newbie likely 'leeching' — better experience routing them elsewhere.
psychology_notes: High-level players don't mind helping low — but ONLY when they chose to. Forced carries breed resentment. Low-level players don't want to feel useless. Tier gates prevent 'I can't play this because I'm not ready' frustration.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player queues with chosen mission tier. Matchmaker scans for similar tier + level. If perfect match, lobby. If carry-pattern detected, suggest different mission to lower-level player.

2. **Math**: Tier hard gate. Level tolerance soft. Carry detection statistical.

3. **Edge cases**: Friend party with level disparity — bypass carry check (they chose this). Solo queue at high tier — fill with bots or restrict.

4. **Visual**: Mission tier selector clear. Level requirement visible. 'Recommended for your level' suggestion.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Tier validation server-side. Level cannot be spoofed.

6. **Rejected**: Random matching across tiers (broken experience), level-strict (impossible to find matches at top).

database_design.schema: coop_queue: player_id, tier, level, party_id, enqueued_at. coop_missions: id, tier, min_level, max_level.

visualization_strategy: Recommended tier highlighted green — clear guidance. Locked tiers grayed out with level requirement shown — sets expectations. Reduced motion: no animations. Color-blind: state indicated by lock icon + text.
balance_considerations: Watch tier mismatch quit rate. Watch carry-rejection rate (target <5% — too high = matching too strict). Watch newbie XP/hr by tier — should grow with progression.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Level spoofing for higher tier — server validates.
Bot leeching — contribution detection in match.
Premade abuse via party of disparate levels — exempted but flagged for review.
observability: Log queues, matches, carry rejections. Metrics: queue time by tier, carry rejection rate, retention by first-co-op-tier-quality.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior allows any-level matching. Level 5 player joins Helldive lobby, dies in 30s, quits game. Helldivers 2 enforces tier gates rigidly for exactly this reason.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Deep Rock Galactic, Helldivers 2. DONE BADLY: Vermintide 2 early — no level matching, newbies in Legend difficulty rage-quit.

game_genre_context: Casual mobile game (Best Fiends, Royal Match) with achievement-driven progression layered on level-based core loop. Achievements give long-term goals beyond 'beat level 5000'.
title: Goal-Setter Achievement System with Tier Customization and 'Suggested Next' Logic
design_intent: Build a system where players SET PERSONAL GOALS from a curated achievement list ('Reach level 100', 'Win 50 matches with X character'), getting bonus rewards for chosen goals. System suggests next goal based on play pattern. This is Duolingo's 'set your daily XP target' applied to achievements — agency drives engagement.
mathematical_foundation: Player picks 1-3 active goals from list of 50+. Goal progress tracked. Bonus reward = base * 2 for goals player chose vs ambient achievements. Suggestion algorithm: rank candidate achievements by predicted_completion_time (close to '2 weeks of player's pace'), avoid recently completed.
psychology_notes: Choosing your goal = commitment. Achievements you 'agreed to' have higher completion. Bonus reward for chosen goals reinforces choice. Suggestions prevent 'choose paradox' for new players.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player browses achievement list, marks 1-3 as 'active goal'. Server tracks progress with 2x bonus tag. Goal completion fires celebration. Suggestion algorithm runs nightly.

2. **Math**: 1-3 goal cap prevents 'set them all' commitment dilution. 2x bonus is psychologically meaningful without breaking economy.

3. **Edge cases**: Player chooses unrealistic goal — completion just delayed, no system intervention. Player abandons goal by un-marking — bonus reverts to base.

4. **Visual**: Active goals pinned at top of achievements list. Progress prominent in HUD. Suggested goals shown with 'Why this?' tooltip.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Bonus calculated server-side on completion.

6. **Rejected**: Auto-assign goals (no agency), unlimited active (commitment dilution).

database_design.schema: Extends achievements with player_goal_selections: player_id, achievement_code, selected_at.

visualization_strategy: Active goals gold-tinted (premium feel from 2x reward). Suggestion cards calmer (slate). Progress bars yellow on active (urgency). 'Add Goal' clearly actionable. Reduced motion: instant progress fill. Color-blind: active state indicated by border + position.
balance_considerations: Watch goal completion rate (target 60%+ — feels achievable). Watch goal-add rate per suggestion (target 25%+ — suggestions resonating). Watch retention by 'has active goal' vs not — should be 2x.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Repeatedly selecting/unselecting to game bonus — bonus only on completion.
Choosing easy goal to farm — bonus pegged to base reward, hard goals still pay more total.
observability: Log goal selected, unselected, completed. Metrics: goal completion rate, suggestion CTR, retention delta.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior auto-assigns goals. Players don't engage. Then: unlimited active — commitment diluted.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Duolingo daily XP target, Apple Fitness Rings. DONE BADLY: 90s MMO 'achievement lists' that just listed everything — analysis paralysis.

game_genre_context: Mobile MOBA / battle royale (Mobile Legends, Brawl Stars, Free Fire) where touchscreen HUD must accommodate joystick + buttons WITHOUT covering important game state. Thumb regions are sacred; off-thumb is fair game.
title: Adaptive Touch HUD with Thumb-Zone Optimization and Reachability Heatmap
design_intent: Build a touch HUD where joystick + action buttons are positioned in thumb-reach zones (within 30% of screen edges), with dynamic resizing for tablets, left/right hand mode toggle, and option for customizable button layout. Brawl Stars allows full customization; PUBG Mobile failed at launch with buttons in unreachable center-screen positions.
mathematical_foundation: Thumb-reach zone: ellipse with major axis = device width * 0.35, minor = device height * 0.45, centered at lower corner. Button hit area: minimum 44pt (Apple HIG), 48dp (Material). Drag-joystick deadzone: 15% of joystick radius (small movements ignored).
psychology_notes: Touch controls are intuitive when ergonomic; frustrating when not. Customizable layout respects player anatomy. Visual feedback on tap (button highlights) confirms input.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: HUD renders with default layout based on device size. Settings allow drag-rearrange. Touch handler maps screen coords to nearest button via Voronoi-like nearest-neighbor.

2. **Math**: Thumb-reach ellipse derived from anatomy studies. Joystick deadzone prevents micro-movements.

3. **Edge cases**: Tablet orientation change — re-layout. Left-handed mode mirrors layout. Notch device — buttons avoid safe areas.

4. **Visual**: Joystick visible only on touch (don't clutter when not aiming). Buttons semi-transparent until used. Customization mode shows drag handles.

5. **Anti-exploit**: HUD client-side only; game actions validated server-side regardless of UI.

6. **Rejected**: Fixed center-screen buttons (unreachable), tiny buttons (mis-tap).

database_design.schema: player_hud_layout: player_id, layout JSONB (button positions), handedness ENUM, scale NUMERIC, updated_at.

visualization_strategy: Joystick semi-transparent until used (de-clutters). Buttons positioned in thumb-arc relative to handedness. Primary attack button largest + red (most-used). Reduced motion: no opacity transitions. Color-blind: action indicated by icon + size, not color.
balance_considerations: Watch button mis-tap rate (target <2%). Watch HUD customization usage (target 30%+ — players DO want to customize). Watch handedness toggle adoption (~10% lefties).
exploit_vectors_addressed: HUD modification for cheat overlay — HUD doesn't expose hidden info, modifications cosmetic only.
Automated input via touch macros — server validates action sequences for human-realistic timing.
observability: Log button taps, layout changes, mis-tap correlations.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior puts buttons in center screen. Players can't reach without re-gripping phone. Or buttons too small — fat-finger mis-taps every match. PUBG Mobile launched with this, fixed in patch 2.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Brawl Stars (impeccable touch), Call of Duty Mobile customization. DONE BADLY: PUBG Mobile launch — buttons too small + poorly placed, mass complaints, urgent patch.

game_genre_context: Web-based loot-box opening (CSGO skin cases, Rocket League blueprints) — the slot-machine-style item reveal is the ENTIRE moment. Players watch reveal videos compulsively. Animation timing IS the dopamine.
title: Loot Box Opening Animation with Suspense Curve and Rarity-Tiered Reveal
design_intent: Build a loot box opening with horizontal scrolling reveal (CSGO-style), suspense curve that slows near the chosen item (the rare moment), particle bursts and screen flash for legendary. The slow-down at the end is the entire psychological mechanism — players KNOW the result is predetermined but the suspense triggers gambling dopamine identically. Valve's CSGO case openings are billion-dollar examples.
mathematical_foundation: Reveal animation: 100 item icons scroll horizontally past a 'selection line', easing function = cubic-bezier(0.1, 0.7, 0.2, 1.0) — fast then very slow at end. Item N selected = settles at position N. Suspense: last 5 items revealed at 0.5s each (heart-pounding pace). Rarity-tiered: legendary triggers extra screen flash + sound.
psychology_notes: Predetermined-but-suspenseful is the slot machine paradigm. Slow reveal of WHICH item triggers anticipation. Rarity-based ceremony rewards investment. The 'unboxing video' culture proves these moments are valuable beyond the item.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player opens box. Server determines item (server-authoritative loot). Client receives result, generates random 'distractor' items, builds scroll animation that stops on chosen item. Reveal sequence plays.

2. **Math**: Suspense curve uses cubic-bezier easing — last 10% of animation is 50% of duration.

3. **Edge cases**: Animation skipped — instant reveal still grants item. Network failure mid-animation — server already granted; client refetches inventory.

4. **Visual**: Horizontal carousel of 100 items scrolls past. Highlight indicator at center. Slow-down at end. Final card pops with rarity-appropriate glow + particles.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Loot determined server-side BEFORE animation. Client animation cosmetic only.

6. **Rejected**: Instant reveal (no ceremony), client-determined result (cheatable), no skip option (frustrates returning players).

database_design.schema: loot_box_openings: id, player_id, box_id, item_id, opened_at, was_skip BOOLEAN.
database_design.transactions: Server determines item + grants atomically; animation is post-hoc display.

visualization_strategy: Selection line is yellow, glowing — focal anchor. Scroll uses heavy ease-out — last items crawl, suspense. Reveal flash white-out for legendary. Rarity color permeates final card. Reduced motion: skip animation entirely, instant reveal. Color-blind: rarity also indicated by border thickness + label.
balance_considerations: Watch skip rate (target 20-30% on repeated openings). Watch share rate per rarity. Animation duration: 4.5s is sweet spot — shorter loses suspense, longer feels boring after 10 boxes.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Predicting reveal via animation — server determines first, animation cosmetic.
Skip to retry — opening committed before animation.
Replay opening to fake high rarity — single-use server transaction.
observability: Log box_opened (with rarity), skip rate. Metrics: rarity distribution, time spent per opening.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior shows instant reveal — no dopamine. Then: client-determined item — modders give themselves legendaries. CSGO case opening generates billions; the animation IS the product.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: CSGO case openings, Hearthstone pack openings. DONE BADLY: many F2P games with 'pop-up reveal' — no suspense, opening boxes feels mechanical, monetization tanks.

game_genre_context: Mobile competitive game (PUBG Mobile, Call of Duty Mobile) where rooted/jailbroken devices enable cheats unavailable on stock devices. Detection of compromised devices is layer 1 of mobile anti-cheat.
title: Mobile Device-Integrity Check with SafetyNet/DeviceCheck and Cheat-Tool Detection
design_intent: Build a device-integrity verification system using Google SafetyNet/Play Integrity API + Apple DeviceCheck, detecting rooted/jailbroken devices, common cheat tools (Game Guardian, Cheat Engine for mobile), and emulators. Compromised devices forced into separate matchmaking pool. PUBG Mobile uses this; ignoring it = unplayable on stock devices.
mathematical_foundation: Integrity score combines: rooted/jailbroken (binary), cheat tool detected (binary), emulator detected (binary), packet timing anomaly. Score >0 = restricted pool. Real-time check on app launch + periodic re-check during match.
psychology_notes: Honest players quit games full of cheaters. Compromised-device pool segregation preserves PvP integrity for stock-device players. Cheaters segregated may STILL pay (cheaters often spend more) — don't ban them, sandbox them.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: App launch → SafetyNet/DeviceCheck attestation → server validates. Match start → re-check. Compromised device → restricted pool (matched only with each other).

2. **Math**: Integrity binary signals AND'd; periodic re-check catches mid-session tampering.

3. **Edge cases**: Carrier-modified Android (some can't pass SafetyNet) — soft-allow with monitoring. Custom ROM enthusiasts (not cheaters) — same pool but flagged for behavior review.

4. **Visual**: Restricted players see normal UI but matchmade only with same pool. No 'YOU'RE BANNED' label.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Attestation tokens server-validated against Google/Apple. Replay attacks blocked via nonce.

6. **Rejected**: Hard-ban rooted devices (community backlash), no detection (cheater overrun), reveal restriction status (cheaters iterate).

database_design.schema: device_integrity_checks: device_id, player_id, check_at, safety_net_result, root_detected, cheat_tool_detected, emulator_detected. player_match_pool: player_id, pool ENUM(main, restricted), reason, set_at.
database_design.denormalization: Pool cached in Redis for fast match-time lookup.

visualization_strategy: No visualization to player — silent restriction.
balance_considerations: Watch false-positive rate (legitimate rooted developers, custom ROMs). Watch restricted-pool match quality.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Rooted device cheats — restricted pool.
Cheat tool injection — process detection.
Emulator-based macros — build fingerprint detection.
Attestation token replay — nonce-bound, single-use.
Integrity API spoofing — server validates with Google/Apple.
observability: Log integrity checks with verdict. Metrics: restricted pool size, false-positive appeal rate.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior trusts client report 'I'm not rooted'. Cheaters say no, server believes. PUBG Mobile early — 30% of matches had at least one cheater.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: PUBG Mobile after S5, Call of Duty Mobile. DONE BADLY: Free Fire early — no integrity check, cheating epidemic, regional bans.

game_genre_context: Hyper-casual mobile game (Subway Surfers, Stack) with weekly tournament events. Quest system rotates weekly tournaments giving cosmetic prizes. Engagement comes from the limited-time event drama.
title: Weekly Tournament Quest with Live Leaderboard and Time-Bound Cosmetic Rewards
design_intent: Build a weekly tournament quest system where players complete tournament-themed quests for tournament-only currency, spend it on tournament-exclusive cosmetics, with a global leaderboard showing top earners. Subway Surfers' weekly hunt is the gold standard — limited-time hype drives DAU. The cosmetics become collectibles ('I was there for tournament 47').
mathematical_foundation: Tournament currency: earned 1:1 from quest completion (linear, transparent). Top 1% global = exclusive cosmetic; top 10% = lesser exclusive. Tournament duration: exactly 7 days, server-enforced start/end. Cosmetics never re-issued (FOMO scarcity).
psychology_notes: Time-limited events drive DAU more than perpetual systems. 'I was there' cosmetics create veteran identity. Top-percent reward gives skilled players something to chase.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: New tournament starts weekly. Players complete daily quests for tournament currency. Spend on tournament-exclusive cosmetics OR save for top-bracket pool. Top % cosmetics auto-distributed on tournament end.

2. **Math**: Linear earn rate transparent; players know exactly how much grinding = which cosmetic.

3. **Edge cases**: Player joins mid-week — pro-rated nothing, fair-play stays. Currency expires at tournament end — converted to soft currency to prevent stockpiling.

4. **Visual**: Tournament hub with countdown timer, exclusive cosmetics gallery, leaderboard tab. Earned-currency prominent. Cosmetics that 'expire from purchase' shown with timer.

5. **Anti-exploit**: All currency server-side. Leaderboard validated.

6. **Rejected**: Perpetual events (no urgency), re-issuing cosmetics (kills exclusivity).

database_design.schema: tournaments: id, code, theme, starts_at, ends_at, cosmetic_pool JSONB. player_tournament_currency: player_id, tournament_id, amount. tournament_purchases: player_id, tournament_id, cosmetic_id, purchased_at.
database_design.transactions: Purchase deducts currency + grants cosmetic atomically.

visualization_strategy: Purple-fuchsia theme = special-event flavor. Countdown timer prominent. Tournament currency icon distinctive. Top 1% gold-highlighted in leaderboard. Reduced motion: no transitions. Color-blind: rank tier via text + position.
balance_considerations: Watch DAU spike per tournament — events should drive 20-40% lift. Watch top-1% pursuit (max-grinder spend rate). Watch tournament-cosmetic re-engagement rate ('I want next week's cosmetic').
exploit_vectors_addressed: Currency manipulation — server-side only.
Leaderboard inflation — earn validation tied to actual gameplay events.
Multi-account farming — device + behavior fingerprint detection.
observability: Log earn events, purchases, end distribution. Metrics: DAU per tournament, conversion to cosmetic purchase, top 1% retention.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior runs perpetual currency event — no urgency, no DAU spike. Then: re-issues cosmetics — veterans angry that 'their' item now common.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Subway Surfers world tour, Fortnite weekly events. DONE BADLY: countless mobile clones running 'events' with permanent rewards — engagement flat.

game_genre_context: Card battler / TCG (Hearthstone, Marvel Snap) with crafting economy — dust dismantling and crafting cards. The crafting system is the F2P path to specific cards without RNG luck.
title: Card Dust Crafting Economy with Disenchant-Rate Curves and Anti-Whale Cap
design_intent: Build a card-dismantle-to-dust → craft-target-card economy with disenchant rates of 25% craft cost (Hearthstone formula), with a daily craft cap to prevent whales burning thousands of cards into dust + crafting an entire collection (devalues the pull economy). Hearthstone perfected this; many clones get the ratios wrong and break either the pack economy or the dust economy.
mathematical_foundation: Disenchant rate = 0.25 (industry standard — 4 dismantled cards = 1 crafted). Craft costs by rarity: Common 40, Rare 100, Epic 400, Legendary 1600. Disenchant gives 25% back. Daily craft cap: 5 legendaries OR equivalent dust value per day. Duplicate-protection: max 2 of any non-Legendary card; further duplicates auto-dust.
psychology_notes: Dust system gives F2P a deterministic path to specific cards — removes RNG luck barrier. 25% disenchant rate balances 'destroy what I don't want' vs 'don't make crafting trivial'. Daily cap prevents 'whale buys 1000 packs, dismantles, crafts perfect deck in 1 day' which devalues other players' collections.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player dismantles unwanted card → receives dust. Player crafts wanted card → spends dust. Daily cap enforced server-side. Duplicates auto-dust.

2. **Math**: 4:1 ratio is psychologically tolerable (player feels 'I'm losing some value' but not robbed). Cap of 5 legendaries/day allows progress but not instant power.

3. **Edge cases**: Player dismantles card they need (mistake) — 1-hour undo window. Card permanently removed from game — dust value preserved.

4. **Visual**: Crafting UI shows dust balance, target card cost, daily-cap progress bar. Dismantle animation: card shatters into dust particles.

5. **Anti-exploit**: All transactions server-side. Cap enforced atomically.

6. **Rejected**: 100% disenchant (no value lost = packs worthless), no cap (whales break economy), no duplicate protection (full collection takes 10x packs).

database_design.schema: player_dust: player_id, amount BIGINT. player_card_inventory: player_id, card_code, count INT. craft_log: id, player_id, action ENUM(craft, disenchant), card_code, dust_delta, at. daily_craft_cap_tracking: player_id, date, crafts_count INT.

visualization_strategy: Rarity color borders cards. Dust balance prominent + tabular. Daily cap progress visible — players aware of limit. Disabled craft button shows reason. Reduced motion: no animations. Color-blind: rarity indicated by border thickness + label.
balance_considerations: Watch dust earn/spend ratio — should hover near 1:1 over weeks (players craft what they want). Watch craft cap hit rate (target <20% of active players — cap shouldn't hit most). Whale cap hits 100% — that's the system working.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Disenchanting then crafting to game — 25% rate ensures dust loss.
Multi-account dust farming — caps per account, fingerprint detection.
Cap manipulation via timezone — server time only.
observability: Log all craft/disenchant. Metrics: dust velocity, cap hit rate, F2P collection completion rate.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: 100% disenchant rate. Packs worthless overnight. Then: no cap. Whales craft full collection day 1. F2P abandon (no pursuit). Hearthstone clones have died on this exact mistake.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Hearthstone arcane dust. DONE BADLY: many TCG clones with no rate balance or no cap, economies collapse.

game_genre_context: Mobile idle clicker (Cookie Clicker, AdVenture Capitalist) with prestige leaderboards. Players compete on 'highest prestige reached', updated less frequently than action-game leaderboards but with extreme number scales (10^200).
title: Big-Number Leaderboard with Scientific-Notation Display and Prestige Tier Brackets
design_intent: Build a leaderboard for idle games where numbers exceed 10^15 (JavaScript number limit), using BigInt server-side and notation strings ('1.45e18 cookies') client-side. Bracket leaderboards by 'prestige tier' so a tier-3 player doesn't compete hopelessly with a tier-50 player. AdVenture Capitalist's 'Mega Bucks' leaderboard is the inspiration.
mathematical_foundation: Numbers stored as strings (mantissa + exponent: '1.45e18') in DB. Sort by exponent first, then mantissa. Prestige tier from log10(total_prestige_progress). Display formatted via BigDecimal-style abbreviations: K/M/B/T/Qa/Qi/Sx/Sp/Oc/No/Dc, then scientific notation.
psychology_notes: Big numbers are the game. Seeing '1.45 nonillion' feels OBSCENE — emotional impact. Tier brackets prevent demoralization (no tier-3 sees tier-50's 10^120).
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player prestiges. Score updated server-side using BigInt. Stored as string in leaderboard. Tier bracket query filters relevant range.

2. **Math**: BigInt operations server-side; client receives notation string only.

3. **Edge cases**: Number overflows BigInt — shouldn't happen at human play rates but capped to 10^400 just in case. Mantissa precision lost beyond 15 digits — acceptable for display.

4. **Visual**: Score in big-number notation ('45.2 quadrillion'). Tier bracket clearly labeled. Player's score position in bracket prominent.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Score validated against expected curve (prestige speed has theoretical max).

6. **Rejected**: JavaScript number storage (overflow at 2^53), client-side computation (cheat magnet).

database_design.schema: player_prestige_score: player_id, tier INT, mantissa NUMERIC, exponent INT, updated_at. (mantissa, exponent) stored separately for efficient sort.
database_design.denormalization: Top 100 per tier cached in Redis ZSET (score = exponent * 1000 + mantissa for unified sort).
database_design.transactions: Score update atomic with audit log.

visualization_strategy: Tier selector horizontal scroll. Player's tier highlighted cyan. Big numbers in yellow with tabular-nums for column alignment. Reduced motion: instant. Color-blind: player position via ring + position.
balance_considerations: Watch tier population — empty tiers indicate progression gates. Watch tier hop frequency.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Big-number overflow exploits — BigInt server-side.
Score injection — server-validated against prestige cadence.
observability: Log score updates, tier changes.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior stores as JS number — overflows at 9 quadrillion (2^53). Player scores wrap to negative. Then: single global leaderboard — new players see 10^120 scores, quit immediately.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: AdVenture Capitalist, Cookie Clicker. DONE BADLY: many idle games with int overflow bugs that wiped progress.

game_genre_context: MMO with guild war / siege mechanics (Lineage 2, Black Desert, Lost Ark). Guild vs guild combat is the endgame. Scheduling wars across timezones, scoring sieges, and preventing dropout-griefing are unsolved problems for many games.
title: Guild War Declaration System with Time-Window Validation and Sandbagging Detection
design_intent: Build a guild war system where guilds declare on each other within scheduled windows (every guild picks 3 preferred war times per week), wars score by objectives held, with sandbagging detection (top guilds intentionally lowering rank to face weak opponents). Lost Ark guild raids and Black Desert sieges have struggled with scheduling and matchmaking; getting it right unlocks the endgame.
mathematical_foundation: War windows: each guild picks 3 time slots from a 21-slot weekly grid (UTC + 30-min granularity). Two guilds with overlapping slot get matched. War score: sum of objective points captured + held duration. Sandbagging: if guild's average member rank drops >20% week-over-week and they declare war on weak guild, flag for review. War rating: ELO-style on war wins.
psychology_notes: Scheduled wars give players appointment-style anticipation (raid night vibe). Sandbagging detection preserves competitive integrity. ELO-style rating creates climbing motivation.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Guild declares war via UI (selects target, time window). System validates target available in slot. War scheduled. At time, server runs siege logic. Score tallied. Rating updated.

2. **Math**: Time-slot overlap simple set intersection. War score formula tunable per game.

3. **Edge cases**: Guild disbands mid-week — wars forfeited. War declared on guild with 0 active members — auto-decline option.

4. **Visual**: War calendar showing scheduled + pending. Live war scoreboard. Post-war replay/MVP.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Sandbagging detection. War rating non-resettable.

6. **Rejected**: Open-anytime wars (no anticipation), pure RNG matchmaking (sandbag-prone).

database_design.schema: guild_war_slots: guild_id, weekly_slot SMALLINT[], updated_at. guild_war_declarations: id, attacker_id, defender_id, scheduled_at, status, score_a, score_b, winner. guild_war_rating: guild_id, rating NUMERIC, war_count INT.

visualization_strategy: 21-slot weekly grid clear. Selected slots highlighted cyan. Schedule shows player's local time. Reduced motion. Color-blind: selection state via fill + position.
balance_considerations: Watch war participation rate per slot — empty slots indicate poor scheduling.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Sandbag rating-drop for easy wars — detection.
Schedule sniping (declare wars at opponent's worst time) — opponent's slot prefs respected.
Forfeiting wars to deny rating — forfeit costs rating equivalent to loss.
observability: Log declarations, results, sandbagging flags.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: open-anytime wars. Top guilds online 24/7, dominate. Then: no sandbagging check, top guilds reset and stomp newbies.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: WoW guild ranked PvP, Lineage 2 sieges. DONE BADLY: BDO node wars early — scheduling chaos.

game_genre_context: Survival game (Rust, DayZ, Ark) where loot drops from killed players + raid-able bases. Distinct from RPG loot — no rarity, just gear. Risk-vs-reward is the core loop.
title: Player-Drop Loot with Time-Based Decay and Risk-Reward Visibility
design_intent: Build player-drop loot system: dying drops your inventory at death location as a 'bag' visible to all players, decaying in 30 minutes. High-value drops create 'loot rush' moments where multiple players converge. Rust's gravestone system was the inspiration; ARK's loot bags are the polished evolution.
mathematical_foundation: Bag despawn: 30 min default, 60 min for high-value (auto-detect via gear score). Visibility: all players within 200m via icon, with rough position. Decay: items rot 5% per minute after timer expires.
psychology_notes: Visible high-value drops create emergent player gatherings (organic events). Time pressure forces decisions. Risk-reward visible — players see WHO died (other-player's gear quality predicts danger of looting).
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player dies → inventory dropped as bag entity at location. Bag broadcasts existence to nearby players. Players who reach can loot. Bag despawns at timer.

2. **Math**: Despawn timer balances loot recovery for owner vs anti-griefing (no eternal bags).

3. **Edge cases**: Player reconnects to corpse — can attempt retrieval (race vs other players). Disconnected before death — items preserved. Bag dropped in inaccessible terrain — server detects, relocates slightly.

4. **Visual**: Bag icon on minimap. Gear-quality glow color (white=common, gold=epic). Despawn timer visible on hover.

5. **Anti-exploit**: All bag creation server-authoritative. Multi-account 'drop-and-retrieve' detected via pattern.

6. **Rejected**: Permanent bags (cluttered world), invisible bags (no risk-reward).

database_design.schema: loot_bags: id, server_id, x, y, z, items JSONB, dropped_by, dropped_at, despawn_at, gear_score INT.
database_design.transactions: Bag drop atomic with inventory clear.

visualization_strategy: Glow color signals gear value at distance — risk-vs-reward read instantly. Bag pulses subtly as despawn nears (urgency). Reduced motion: no pulse. Color-blind: bag value via icon size + label on hover.
balance_considerations: Watch loot-bag claim rate by gear tier (target 80%+ of high-value claimed within 5 min). Watch griefing rate (players camping spawns for bags).
exploit_vectors_addressed: Multi-account drop-loot — pattern detection of same-IP drop/recover.
Inventory dupe via crash on death — atomic transaction.
Bag spawn manipulation — fixed at death location.
observability: Log bag drops, claims, despawns. Metrics: average bag lifetime, value-tier distribution.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: no decay — world cluttered with bags. Then: invisible bags — no risk-reward dynamic. Then: bags only visible to owner — defeats survival genre.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Rust gravestones, ARK loot bags. DONE BADLY: many survival clones with broken loot persistence.

game_genre_context: Hero shooter with deep cosmetic economy (Overwatch 2, Valorant). Season pass intersects with limited-time bundles. Pass tier rewards must integrate with bundle marketplace.
title: Season Pass + Limited Bundle Cross-Promotion with Anti-FOMO Discount Logic
design_intent: Build a season pass that integrates with limited-time bundles, with logic that prevents FOMO-double-purchase: if player owns the pass, bundles auto-discount by overlap percentage; if player buys bundle items they got from pass, they get currency refund. Valorant got this right; Overwatch 2 launched without this and players ragequit at duplicate purchase.
mathematical_foundation: Overlap calculation: for each bundle item, check if player owns via pass. Discount = sum(owned_item_value) / bundle_value. Refund on bundle purchase: if any bundle item duplicate, refund equivalent premium currency.
psychology_notes: Players HATE buying things they already own. Auto-discount + refund proves the company isn't predatory.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player browses bundle. Server checks player's pass progress + owned cosmetics. Discounts bundle accordingly. Purchase grants new items + refund for duplicates.

2. **Math**: Discount calculation per bundle per player at view time.

3. **Edge cases**: Player owns all bundle items — bundle hidden. Player buys bundle then pass — pass items already-owned excluded from grants.

4. **Visual**: Bundle shows base price, discounted price for player, with explanation tooltip ('you save X because you own Y').

5. **Anti-exploit**: Discount computed server-side per-player.

6. **Rejected**: Fixed bundle price (FOMO predator).

database_design.schema: bundle_purchases: player_id, bundle_id, paid_price, refund_amount, purchased_at. Extends cosmetic ownership tracking.
database_design.denormalization: Player-specific bundle price cached in Redis for 5 min.

visualization_strategy: Strikethrough original price + discount badge — savings visible. Owned-items explanation builds trust. Reduced motion: no animations.
balance_considerations: Watch bundle conversion rate per discount tier. Watch refund frequency.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Re-purchase exploit — bundle purchase idempotent.
Refund manipulation — refund only for verified duplicates.
observability: Log bundle views, purchases, discounts applied, refunds. Metrics: per-bundle conversion, average discount.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior sells bundle at fixed price. Players who own 80% of items via pass refuse to buy. Or worse, buy and rage-refund. Overwatch 2 launch did this.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Valorant bundle smart-discount. DONE BADLY: Overwatch 2 launch (since fixed).

game_genre_context: Real-time strategy game (StarCraft, Age of Empires) with command queues per unit, lockstep simulation for fairness. Lockstep ensures all clients compute same state from same inputs — no desync.
title: Lockstep RTS Simulation with Deterministic Random and Input Queue Buffering
design_intent: Build a lockstep RTS where all clients receive same input events at same simulation tick, run identical deterministic simulation locally. Server is input-broker, not state-arbiter. Input buffer absorbs network jitter. StarCraft, AoE, Warcraft all use lockstep — perfect fairness, replay-friendly, but desync = match aborted.
mathematical_foundation: Sim tick rate: 10Hz (slower than action games — RTS doesn't need 60Hz). Input buffer: 100ms ahead — gives time for inputs to propagate. Deterministic PRNG seeded at match start (same on all clients). Floating-point ops avoided (use integers or fixed-point) — different CPUs round differently.
psychology_notes: RTS players accept slight input lag (100ms) for guaranteed fairness. Lockstep replays let players study matches.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Each tick, server collects all client inputs for tick N+10 (100ms ahead), broadcasts. Clients buffer until tick N+10 reached, apply, advance sim. State desync detection via hash compare.

2. **Math**: Integer math for determinism. PRNG via xorshift seeded at match start.

3. **Edge cases**: Client misses tick — wait or speed-up locally. Desync detected — match abort, replay generated.

4. **Visual**: 100ms input lag invisible at 10Hz sim. Smooth interpolation hides discrete ticks.

5. **Anti-exploit**: All sim deterministic. Maphack impossible because hidden info not sent.

6. **Rejected**: Server-authoritative state (heavy server load), float math (desync nightmare).

database_design.schema: matches: id, start_seed, players UUID[]. tick_inputs: match_id, tick INT, player_id, input JSONB.

visualization_strategy: 100ms input lag invisible at 10Hz tick. Smooth interpolation between ticks. Reduced motion: instant unit movement.
balance_considerations: Watch desync rate (target <0.1%). Watch average input lag (target 100-150ms).
exploit_vectors_addressed: Maphack — clients have full state but display layer enforces fog-of-war; modified clients possible but produce desync.
Speedhack — server controls tick rate.
Input forgery — server validates input belongs to player.
observability: Log desyncs, tick latency. Metrics: per-match desync rate.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior uses float positions — different CPUs desync within 1 minute. Match aborts. Then: no input buffer — laggy player causes everyone to wait.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: StarCraft 2, Age of Empires II. DONE BADLY: many indie RTS shipped with float ops, constant desyncs.

game_genre_context: Competitive ladder game (StarCraft, chess.com) with per-match analysis showing build orders, decision points, where the match was won/lost. Post-match analysis is the second game for serious players.
title: Per-Match Replay Analysis with Key-Moment Detection and Heatmap Generation
design_intent: Build a post-match analysis tool that auto-identifies key moments (gold spike, ace, comeback), generates positional heatmap of player presence, and tags the moment-of-defeat ('you lost map control at 12:34'). Chess.com's analysis is the model — every move scored, mistakes highlighted. LoL's post-game graphs do this for gold/XP diffs.
mathematical_foundation: Key moment detection: derivative spikes in score/gold/objective. Heatmap: 2D histogram of player positions per minute, smoothed via Gaussian blur. Moment-of-defeat: largest gold/objective swing in opponent's favor with no recovery.
psychology_notes: Self-analysis drives improvement. 'Why did I lose' answered visually = teaching moment. Heatmap reveals tendencies players don't know about themselves ('I always camp top lane').
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Match ends → replay data uploaded to S3. Analysis worker processes: scans for moments, builds heatmap, generates summary. Stored in player_match_analysis.

2. **Math**: Moment detection via local maxima of swing-derivative. Heatmap binned 50x50 over map.

3. **Edge cases**: Insufficient replay data — partial analysis. Spectator-only matches — analysis still generated.

4. **Visual**: Match timeline with annotated moments. Heatmap overlay on map. Key-moment 'jump-to' clips.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Analysis from replay data only.

6. **Rejected**: Live-streaming analysis (too late), text-only summary (less engaging).

database_design.schema: match_replays: id, match_id, s3_url, duration. player_match_analysis: id, match_id, player_id, key_moments JSONB, heatmap JSONB, moment_of_defeat JSONB.

visualization_strategy: Timeline with color-coded moments (green spike, red loss). Defeat moment called out in dramatic rose card. Heatmap red-intensity shows player presence. Reduced motion: static. Color-blind: moment type via icon + position.
balance_considerations: Watch analysis engagement (post-match views). Watch improvement correlation (do users who view analysis improve faster?).
exploit_vectors_addressed: Replay tampering — replays signed by server during match.
Heatmap-based stalking — own-player only display by default.
observability: Log analysis_viewed, replay_replayed. Metrics: engagement, retention correlation.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: stat dump. Boring. Then: no key moments — players don't know what to focus on. Then: heatmap of all 10 players (too dense). Chess.com analysis is gold for a reason.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: chess.com, LoL post-game. DONE BADLY: many shooters with bare stats — no insight, no improvement loop.

game_genre_context: PvP fighting game (Street Fighter, Tekken) with regional matchmaking — netcode quality matters MORE than rating quality. A 50ms ping match vs lower-skilled is better than 200ms vs equal-skilled.
title: Latency-First Fighting Game Matchmaker with Rollback Netcode Suitability Test
design_intent: Build a matchmaker for fighting games that prioritizes ping over rating (under 80ms preferred), tests rollback-netcode suitability via initial ping-pong, falls back to wider rating range only if no good-ping opponent. Tekken 8 and Street Fighter 6 use this. Bad netcode = stuttery match = uninstall.
mathematical_foundation: Ping target: <80ms preferred, <120ms acceptable. Matchmaking expands rating range as ping pool empties: start ±50 rating + <80ms ping. After 10s: ±100 + <120ms ping. After 30s: any. Suitability test: 10 ping samples, std deviation must be <10ms (stable connection).
psychology_notes: Fighting game players prioritize match QUALITY (netcode) over OPPONENT EQUALITY (rating). One stuttery match drives more rage than one rating-mismatched match.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player queues. Server pings client periodically (or uses ICE candidates for direct connect). Matchmaker scans pool, ranks candidates by ping then rating distance. Tests connection before locking in.

2. **Math**: Std deviation of ping samples reveals connection stability — jittery = unsuitable.

3. **Edge cases**: VPN abuse to appear local — server geo-validates via IP. Late-night low-pop regions — fallback to higher ping.

4. **Visual**: Queue UI shows estimated ping to match. Connection-quality test visible at match start.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Geo-validation prevents VPN-region-jumping for easy matches.

6. **Rejected**: Pure rating matchmaking (laggy matches), no quality test (frustration).

database_design.schema: player_connection_quality: player_id, last_known_ip, region, avg_ping_to_servers JSONB. queue: standard.
database_design.denormalization: Recent ping pool cached in Redis.

visualization_strategy: Ping prominent at match start — quality transparency. Stability test visible. Reduced motion: instant transitions.
balance_considerations: Watch average match ping (target <100ms global). Watch queue time vs ping tradeoff.
exploit_vectors_addressed: VPN region-hop for easy match — geo-validate.
Lag-switch (intentional disconnects to win) — server detects + penalty.
observability: Log match pings, queue durations.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior matchmakes on rating only. 200ms+ matches every queue. Fighting game players uninstall. Tekken 7's notorious launch netcode lost the game its competitive scene for years.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Tekken 8, Street Fighter 6 (GGPO rollback + ping-first MM). DONE BADLY: Tekken 7 launch delay netcode (delay-based, not rollback) — community recovery took 5 years.

game_genre_context: Open-world exploration game (No Man's Sky, Star Citizen, Elite Dangerous) with procedurally generated planets/star systems. Generating BILLIONS of unique places means players keep exploring forever.
title: Seeded Galaxy Generator with Biome Coherence and Discoverable Anomaly Injection
design_intent: Build a procedural galaxy generator producing 18 quintillion star systems (No Man's Sky's number), each with planets, atmospheres, biomes — all deterministic from coordinate seed. Biomes must be COHERENT (you don't want desert+arctic on same planet). Inject rare anomalies (unique planets with hand-crafted content) procedurally placed so players find them as 'discoveries'. NMS is the model; many open-world games fail at procgen scale.
mathematical_foundation: Star system position: (x, y, z) ∈ Int64³ = 18 quintillion possible. Star seed = hash(x ⊕ y ⊕ z). System params (star type, planet count) derived from seed. Per-planet seed = hash(system_seed ⊕ planet_index). Biome coherence via Perlin noise + climate gradients (latitude affects temperature). Anomaly placement: every 1000th system on average via deterministic test.
psychology_notes: Procedural infinity gives 'I could be the first to find this' feeling. Coherent biomes feel believable. Discoverable anomalies inject hand-crafted moments without players knowing which are which.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player jumps to coordinates. Client requests generation. Server computes deterministically. Returns planet data. Player visits planet, biome generated similarly.

2. **Math**: 64-bit seed gives effectively infinite uniqueness. Perlin noise for terrain. Climate model from physics (axial tilt → seasons).

3. **Edge cases**: Player at coordinate edge of int64 — wrap or reject. Coordinate collision (two players same system) — system shared, but their PLANET visits independent.

4. **Visual**: Star system view shows generated planets with shaders. Biome transitions smooth via blending.

5. **Anti-exploit**: All generation server-side or deterministic-client-validatable.

6. **Rejected**: Hand-crafted only (limited content), random everything (incoherent worlds).

database_design.schema: discovered_systems: player_id, x, y, z, discovered_at, named_by, named_to. anomaly_registry: (x, y, z) of placed anomalies + their hand-crafted content_id.

visualization_strategy: Star color matches astrophysics. Planets colored by primary biome. Anomaly badge gold-fuchsia to distinguish. System ID feels like coordinates (immersion). Reduced motion: static.
balance_considerations: Watch discovery rate per player (target steady). Anomaly find rate (target 1 per 50 systems for active explorers).
exploit_vectors_addressed: Coordinate spam to find anomalies — discovery requires actual visit + landing.
Naming abuse — profanity filter on discovered_systems names.
observability: Log generations, discoveries, anomaly finds.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: random biomes per planet. Desert next to arctic on same planet. Immersion broken. Then: not seeded — players can't share coordinates.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: No Man's Sky, Elite Dangerous (Stellar Forge). DONE BADLY: Star Citizen handcrafted approach hit content scaling limits.

game_genre_context: Web-based VR/3D experience (Beat Saber web, Resonite). Particle systems and visual effects are the wow factor. Performant particles in a browser is a difficult constraint.
title: GPU-Instanced Particle System for 10,000 Particles at 60fps with Lifetime Curves
design_intent: Build a particle system using Three.js InstancedMesh for 10k particles at 60fps, with lifetime curves (size+color+velocity changes over time), gravity, and burst spawn modes. Beat Saber's note-shatter particles, every modern web game uses this pattern. CPU-iterated particles cap at ~500; GPU-instanced unlock 10k+.
mathematical_foundation: Per particle: position, velocity, lifetime, age. Each frame: age += dt; position += velocity * dt; velocity += gravity * dt. Size = lerp(startSize, endSize, age/lifetime). Color same. GPU update via instanced attributes; rendered via single draw call.
